title: Conner Robertson
text:
Executive Summary
This market research paper outlines comprehensive strategies aimed at increasing engagement, visibility, and overall fandom for the Wisconsin Timber Rattlers, focusing on sports marketing innovation, digital outreach, and new College Night initiatives. Drawing from extensive social media analytics, national sports marketing trends, and primary survey research, our goal is to modernize how the team connects with key demographics.
By aligning digital content with fan behavior, activating cause-based and community- driven sponsorships, and creating personalized experiences for Gen Z fans, the Timber Rattlers can position themselves as a modern minor league team that fosters loyalty, inclusivity, and excitement. These efforts offer more than short-term attendance boosts— they lay the groundwork for a new generation of lifelong fans.
Social media analysis across TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, and Twitter reveals several trends. Player and Marketing-focused content consistently drives the highest engagement, particularly when posted on weekends and throughout in-season months. TikTok and Reels thrive on trend participation and player personality, while Facebook and Twitter see deeper fan interaction through video content and community-based content. Strategic timing, content format, and cross-platform recycling are a few important steps for growing reach and sustaining fan engagement.
Survey research conducted among Lawrence University students supports a strong interest in the return of College Night, particularly if events are hosted in May and early June. The main barriers - academic conflicts, transportation, and lack of peer presence - can be addressed through thoughtful planning, including school-provided shuttles, student ambassadors, and themed programming. Respondents expressed clear interest in affordable pricing, interactive games, free merch, and school-specific celebrations.
View Our Final Report! PDF Document

title: Conner Robertson
text:
Executive Summary
Unlock Performance Institute is committed to revolutionizing baseball pitching development by creating innovative, technology-based training programs. Its unique methodology, founded on private instruction, leading-edge equipment, and customized programming, enables every athlete at each level to tap into their fullest potential. Passionately fueled with a vision of accessibility, inclusiveness, and excellence, Unlock Performance Institute will make a difference in amateur and collegiate baseball athletes for generations.
Founded to provide top-notch and tailored training to pitchers, our business was established to fill a vital void in the market. Most Division III baseball programs and amateur players have zero access to organized programming and professional-level instruction. By providing customized programs in our Colorado facility and online for college programs across the country, Unlock Performance Institute equips athletes with the means to unlocktheir potential and perform at an unprecedented level.
Our business operates on several primary values. Perseverance, inclusion, innovation, integrity, accessibility, and customization are at the top of our list of priorities in all that we undertake. These enable us to provide an open, efficient, and effective experience for each and every athlete that we assist.
Unlock Performance Institute’s financial projections are for high growth. Our year one revenues are $145,084, increasing consistently to $437,046 in year two and $840,769 in year three. Our fixed-cost model is based on strategic investment in equipment, facilities, and professional coaching to support sustainable operations and deliver excellent value to our clients. We feel that we can grow successfully by careful cash flow management and by leveraging partnerships with sporting organizations.
PDF Document

title: Unlock Performance Institute
text:
PDF Document

Unlock Performance Institute is a technology-driven baseball training company focused on advanced pitching development for high school and collegiate athletes.
UPI was created to fill a gap in amateur baseball training, especially for athletes and Division III programs that often lack access to professional-level resources, structured programming, and advanced performance technology.
The business combines individualized training plans, pitching analytics, strength development, arm care, and remote coaching to help athletes improve performance while reducing injury risk.
UPI’s model is built around accessibility, innovation, and customization, giving athletes a more organized and measurable way to train.
The company plans to grow through in-person training in Colorado, remote programming for athletes nationwide, partnerships with high schools, and long-term consulting relationships with collegiate programs.

title: Business & Entrepreneurship UPI Model
text:
Unlock Performance Institute is a technology-driven baseball training company focused on advanced pitching development for high school and collegiate athletes.
UPI was created to fill a gap in amateur baseball training, especially for athletes and Division III programs that often lack access to professional-level resources, structured programming, and advanced performance technology.
The business combines individualized training plans, pitching analytics, strength development, arm care, and remote coaching to help athletes improve performance while reducing injury risk.
UPI’s model is built around accessibility, innovation, and customization, giving athletes a more organized and measurable way to train.
The company plans to grow through in-person training in Colorado, remote programming for athletes nationwide, partnerships with high schools, and long-term consulting relationships with collegiate programs.

title: Unlock Performance Institute
text:
Business Goals and Objectives

Phase 1: Build Initial Recognition

Begin by developing strong relationships with local high schools, coaches, parents, and athletes.
Host clinics, provide assessments, and offer direct training to establish trust and build brand awareness.
The goal is to secure connections with several local high schools and create an early base of recurring clients.

Phase 2: Establish the Facility

Secure funding and rent an indoor training facility large enough for pitching lanes, strength training, recovery work, and athlete development.
The facility will serve as the foundation for UPI’s in-person training model and create a professional environment for athletes to train consistently.

Phase 3: Build a Reliable Customer Base

Grow the in-facility athlete base by providing consistent programming, measurable results, and strong communication.
UPI aims to create long-term athlete retention by helping players clearly understand their progress and development.

Phase 4: Expand Through Remote Training

Use remote programming to reach athletes outside Colorado.
Athletes will receive assessments, video analysis, individualized plans, and ongoing communication through digital platforms.
This allows UPI to scale beyond the limits of a physical facility.

Phase 5: Partner with Collegiate Programs

Expand into team-wide programming for Division III baseball programs.
UPI will provide structured pitching development, workload management, and performance support for schools that may not have the budget for specialized pitching staff.

title: Unlock Performance Institute
text:
External Market Analysis

BI: Business Implications

To be successful, UPI must build genuine, long-term relationships with athletes, coaches, and parents.
The business depends on trust, measurable results, and a training environment that athletes believe will help them improve.
UPI must also maintain consistent program quality, keep up with industry advancements, and retain athletes by regularly updating training systems and staying connected with its community.

KLP: Key Leverage Point

UPI can use seasonality as an advantage rather than a weakness.
During the baseball season, many competitors experience lower training demand. UPI can use this time to improve programming, build relationships, run clinics, and prepare athletes for off-season development.
By staying active during slower periods, UPI can position itself as the preferred training option when athletes are ready to return to structured development.

SCA: Sustainable Competitive Advantage

UPI’s strongest competitive advantage is its detailed integration of advanced technology with individualized pitching development.
By using tools such as Trackman, Rapsodo, Driveline, and Arm Care, UPI can provide data-driven training that is more specific and measurable than traditional coaching.
This allows the company to personalize each athlete’s plan, track progress, improve performance, and reduce injury risk.
UPI’s focus on pitchers, combined with its technology-based training model and ability to serve both individuals and teams, creates a unique position in the baseball development market.

title: Unlock Performance Institute
text:
PDF Document

Unlock Performance Institute is a technology-driven baseball training company focused on advanced pitching development for high school and collegiate athletes.
UPI was created to fill a gap in amateur baseball training, especially for athletes and Division III programs that often lack access to professional-level resources, structured programming, and advanced performance technology.
The business combines individualized training plans, pitching analytics, strength development, arm care, and remote coaching to help athletes improve performance while reducing injury risk.
UPI’s model is built around accessibility, innovation, and customization, giving athletes a more organized and measurable way to train.
The company plans to grow through in-person training in Colorado, remote programming for athletes nationwide, partnerships with high schools, and long-term consulting relationships with collegiate programs.

title: Unlock Performance Institute
text:
Financial Analysis & Plan

UPI’s financial plan focuses on securing enough startup funding to launch the facility, purchase necessary equipment, and create a strong foundation for long-term growth.
The business requires $275,000 in financing, which covers the most important startup needs:



Facility rent for 2 years: $150,000
Equipment costs: $106,199
Additional startup costs and buffer: about $18,801



Projected revenue shows strong growth over the first three years:



Year 1: $157,500
Year 2: $337,625
Year 3: $651,500


The first year is expected to involve heavy reinvestment and lower profitability because of startup costs. However, by years two and three, UPI expects stronger cash flow as the client base grows, services expand, and the business becomes more established.
A few key financial indicators support the company’s long-term potential. UPI projects an average gross profit margin of 49%, an average return on assets of 21%, and an average return on equity of 38% over the first three years. These numbers suggest that, once the business moves past the startup phase, it has the potential to use its assets efficiently and generate strong returns.
The main financial priority is managing cash effectively. UPI expects strong liquidity, but the plan also recognizes that holding too much unused cash could be inefficient. As the business grows, extra cash should be reinvested into equipment, technology, facility improvements, marketing, or investor returns.

title: SQL Project
text:
GitHub Repo
-- creating a temporary ta ble 'joined_data'
-- technically a 'CTE'
-- using this later for second part of queery
WITH joined_data AS (
    SELECT
        p.Season,       
        p.team_name,    
        p.ERA,          
        f.OAA,          -- Outs Above Average
        f.DRS           -- Defensive Runs Saved
    FROM team_pitching AS p
    JOIN team_fielding AS f
        ON p.Season = f.Season
       AND p.team_name = f.team_name
    WHERE p.Season = 2024
        -- Keep only rows from the 2024 season.
    AND f.OAA IS NOT NULL),
        -- Removing rows where OAA is missing
-- now creating a second temporary table
condition_data AS (
    SELECT
        Season,
        team_name,
        ERA,
        OAA,
        DRS,
        CASE
            -- CASE works like an if/else statement in SQL
            WHEN
                (CASE WHEN OAA >= 25 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) +
                (CASE WHEN DRS >= 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 2
            THEN 'Excellent Defense'
                -- conditions must meet OAA at least 25 and DRS at least 5
                -- Each inner CASE returns 1 if the condition is true
                -- and 0 if it is false
                -- so if the two values add up to 2, both conditions were true and
                -- that team will retain "Excelletnt Degense" in the defense_tier variable
            WHEN
                (CASE WHEN OAA <= 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) +
                (CASE WHEN DRS <= 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 2

            THEN 'Poor Defense'
                -- same thing as before,
                -- each condition returns either 1 or 0.
                -- A total of 2 means both poor-defense conditions were true.

            ELSE 'Mediocre Defense'
                -- Mediocre Defense when neither condition is true
                -- So any team that is not Excellent and not Poor becomes
                -- Mediocre Defense
        END AS defense_tier
            -- naming new column as "defense_tier"
    FROM joined_data)
-- selecting the final columns for visualization
SELECT
    Season,
    team_name,
    ERA,
    defense_tier,
    CASE
        -- with this CASE statement, creating order for the defensive categories
        WHEN defense_tier = 'Poor Defense' THEN 1
        WHEN defense_tier = 'Mediocre Defense' THEN 2
        WHEN defense_tier = 'Excellent Defense' THEN 3
    END AS defense_order
        -- naming new listing column as defense_order
FROM condition_data
    -- using conditional data from earlier
ORDER BY defense_order, ERA;
The plot below orders teams by ERA, so teams farther left have better run prevention and teams farther right have worse run prevention. The colors represent defensive tiers that were created by combining each team’s OAA (Outs Above Average) and DRS (Defensive Runs Saved) values for the 2024 season. To make these groups, I first joined the 2024 team pitching and team fielding tables so that each row represented one team with both its ERA and defensive metrics. Then, using OAA and DRS, teams were classified into three groups: teams that performed strongly on both defensive measures were labeled Excellent Defense, teams that rated poorly on both were labeled Poor Defense, and teams that were mixed or near the middle were labeled Mediocre Defense. (“High” OAA >= 25, DRS >= 5, “Poor” OAA >= 0, DRS >= 0, and anything being else being considered “Mediocre”.
From the visualization, we can see that there is a somewhat positive relationship between the overal defensive performance for a team in 2024 and their overall ERA. Teams in the excellent are split, with half being in the lower range of ERA’s and other half being in the higher range. However, more clearly, teams with a “Mediocre” (decent) defensive year tend to appear more often on the lower-ERA side, suggesting better defense is associated with better pitching outcomes. With that, it is important to note that poor defensive teams held mid-to-high ERAs, which is to drastic to completely ignore. But the colors are still fairly mixed across the plot, so defense alone of course does not fully encapsolate a team’s ERA for a given year.
Another reason for this is shown in that the mediocre defense group mostly covers a very wide range of ERA values, meaning average defensive teams were not all the same in run prevention. furher demonstrating how factors outside of defense are vitally important: like pitching talent, strikeouts, walks, home runs allowed, etc.

# plotting ERA by team, ordered by ERA and colored by defensive tier
p1 <- ggplot(defensive_era_analysis, 
             aes(reorder(team_name, ERA), ERA, color = defense_tier)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(xend = reorder(team_name, ERA), y = 0, yend = ERA),
    linewidth = 0.7,
    alpha = 0.7) +
  
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Excellent Defense" = "#457B9D",
      "Mediocre Defense" = "#A8DADC",
      "Poor Defense" = "#E63946")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Team ERA Compared to OAA and BRS Defensive Tiers",
    subtitle = "Defensive Tiers Calculated By OAA and DRS Averages",
    x = "Team Average OAA and DRS Tiers",
    y = "ERA",
    color = "Defense tier") +
  theme_grey() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.background = element_rect(color ="darkgrey"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(color = "darkgrey"),
    axis.text.y = element_blank())
